MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 2
Chapter 5 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 2 – Part 2
5.2 Identify and discuss the stages in the buyer decision process.

Part 2  - The buyer decision process.

The buyer decision process (1 of 5)
Stages in the buyer decision process
The buyer decision process consists of five stages.
Figure 5.5 The buyer decision process

The buyer decision process (2 of 5)
Stages in the buyer decision process (cont.)
Need recognition
The buyer recognises a problem or need. It can be triggered by internal stimuli (e.g. hunger, thirst) or external stimuli (e.g. advertisement).
Information search
The buyer seeks out information about products or services with the potential to satisfy the need.
User-generated content (UGC) is any content, including images, videos, text and audio, voluntarily created and shared online, usually on social media, by individuals, fans or consumers of a brand who are not associated with that brand.

The buyer decision process (3 of 5)
Stages in the buyer decision process (cont.)
Evaluation of alternatives
The consumer processes information to arrive at brand choices.
Marketers should study buyers to find out how they actually evaluate brand alternatives so that they can take steps to assist buyers in their decision making.
Purchase decision
The consumer forms a purchase intention and ultimately makes the purchase.
Two factors can come between the purchase intention and purchase decision: attitudes of others and unexpected situational factors.

The buyer decision process (4 of 5)
Stages in the buyer decision process (cont.)
Post-purchase behaviour
Following the purchase, the consumer will engage in post-purchase behaviour depending on their level of satisfaction or dissatisfaction with the purchase.
Q: Give some examples of post-purchase behaviour.

The buyer decision process (5 of 5)
Stages in the buyer decision process (cont.)
Roles in the buying process
Five different roles can be identified in most buying processes:
initiator – the person who first suggests the idea of buying a particular product or service
influencer – a person whose views or advice carry some weight in making the final buying decision
decider – the person who ultimately makes a buying decision or any part of it (e.g. whether to buy, what to buy, how or where to buy)
buyer – the person who makes an actual purchase
user – the person who consumes or uses the product or service.

The buyer decision process
I am not getting the results I expected from the product so I:
Read the instructions,
Call the advice line number on the product or packaging.
Go to the brand website and search for information.
The product breaks down or malfunctions so I:
Have it repaired or replaced under the warranty or guarantee.
I am very satisfied by the product – it delivered all or more than I believe that I was promised by the marketer. Consequently, I become a Brand Loyal and buy it regularly and tell others about what a great product it is.
Q: Give some examples of post-purchase behaviour.

Learning Objective 2 – now you have learnt how to:
5.2 Identify and discuss the stages in the buyer decision process.
